g.data-narration: Welcome to symmetric cryptography. We explore how a single shared key enables secure communication.
g.text: Secret Key Encryption • AES • Block & Stream Ciphers

g.data-narration: Symmetric cryptography uses one secret key for both encryption and decryption. Both sender and receiver must share this key securely.
g.text: Uses a single shared secret key for both encryption and decryption.
Both parties must share the key through a secure channel beforehand.

g.data-narration: Encryption transforms plaintext into ciphertext using the secret key. Decryption reverses this process to recover the original message.
Symmetric is 100-1000x faster than asymmetric but faces key distribution challenges. Asymmetric solves this with public-private key pairs.
Block ciphers encrypt fixed-size blocks of data. AES uses 128-bit blocks while DES uses 64-bit blocks.
g.text: Encrypt data in fixed-size blocks. Common block sizes: 64-bit (DES), 128-bit (AES).

g.data-narration: Stream ciphers encrypt data one bit or byte at a time using a keystream. ChaCha20 is a modern example used in TLS.
g.text: Encrypt data one bit or byte at a time using a pseudorandom keystream.

g.data-narration: AES is the gold standard for symmetric encryption with 128-bit blocks and key sizes of 128, 192, or 256 bits.
g.text: The most widely used symmetric cipher today. Block size: 128 bits. Key sizes: 128, 192, or 256 bits.

g.data-narration: Each AES round performs four operations: SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, and AddRoundKey.
g.text: 10 rounds for AES-128 • 12 rounds for AES-192 • 14 rounds for AES-256

g.data-narration: AES encryption starts with AddRoundKey, then performs 9 rounds of all four operations, and finishes with the final round.
ECB mode encrypts each block independently. This is insecure because identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, revealing patterns.
g.text: Each block encrypted independently with the same key. Problem: Identical plaintext = identical ciphertext!

g.data-narration: CBC mode chains blocks together by XORing each plaintext with the previous ciphertext. Requires an IV for the first block.
g.text: Each block is XORed with previous ciphertext before encryption. Requires IV (Initialization Vector).

g.data-narration: CTR mode converts block ciphers into stream ciphers using incrementing counters. It can be parallelized for better performance.
g.text: Converts block cipher into stream cipher using incrementing counter. Can be parallelized for speed.

g.data-narration: In blockchain, AES-256 encrypts private keys with a user password. Key derivation functions like PBKDF2 create secure keys.
g.text: Private keys stored in wallets are encrypted using AES-256 with user-provided passphrase.

g.data-narration: Symmetric crypto secures node communication via TLS and protects data in private blockchains.
